---
title: "Documenting software"
subtitle: "CCP-TEPP Docathon 2026"
author: "Andrew Lister"
institute: "UKRI - STFC"
format:
  revealjs:
    navigation-mode: vertical
    theme: serif
toc: true
toc-depth: 1
toc-title: Outline

transition: fade
preview-links: true

from: markdown+emoji
---

# Fundamentals

## Disclaimers

- What counts as "clear documentation" is subjective
- This presentation will focus on C.
  Everything also applies to Python, Fortran, C++, etc.

::: {.notes}
I'll give you my opinions, searching for information on any of these topics
online will yield any number of other opinions that you can consult!
:::

## Importance of good docs

- Understanding what the code is doing
- Give people the confidence to use the software
- Encourage contribution
- Reduce user queries

## Motivating example
::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=1}
$$
\epsilon = \sqrt{\sum_i^n \frac{{\left(\hat{x}_{i} - x_{i}\right)}^2}{n}}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1}
```c
double myfun(int arg1, int *arg2, int *arg3)
{
    double var1 = 0;
    for(int var2 = 0; var2 < arg1; var2++)
    {
        int var3 = arg2[var2] - arg3[var2]
        var1 += var3 * var3 / arg1;
    }
    return sqrt(var1);
}
```
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=3}
::: {style="font-size: 80%"}
```c
/// @brief Compute and return the root mean square (rms) error between
///        an expected value and the actual value.
/// @param n         Length of the vectors
/// @param actual    Computed values to compare
/// @param expected  Values expected from the computation
/// @return          Root mean square error
double rms_error(int n, int *actual, int *expected)
{
    double error = 0; // Running total of errors
    // Add contributions from each index to error
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        int error_i = actual[i] - expected[i];
        error += error_i * error_i / n;
    }
    return sqrt(error);
}
```
:::
:::
:::

## The Diátaxis framework

![The [Diátaxis](https://diataxis.fr/) compass for documentation](https://diataxis.fr/_images/diataxis.png)

## Explanation
::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
### User
- Why use the software?
- Reporting bugs?
:::
::: {.column}
### Developer
- Project structure
- Contributor guidelines (version control, style, testing)
- Method/algorithm notes
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The kind of documentation that makes sense when apart from the rest of the work
:::

## Tutorials
::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
### User
- User workshop
- Lessons
:::
::: {.column}
### Developer
- Hackathon
- How to contribute
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Guided learning with emphasis on the learner doing something
:::

## How-to-guides
::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
### User
- How to use the software
- Example programs/workflows
- Installing the software
:::
::: {.column}
### Developer
- Submitting/Reviewing a PR
- Running the tests
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Recipes for doing a particular task
:::

## Reference
::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
### User
- Syntax
- Function signatures and descriptions
- API
:::
::: {.column}
### Developer
- Code comments
- Useful variable names
- Style guide
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
As descriptive as possible, not something that is generally read front to back
:::

## My suggested priority

1. API/function docs (reference)
2. Getting started - how to install, basic examples (how-to-guides)
3. Overview - why use the software, project structure (explanation)
4. Contributor guidelines, style guide (explanation, reference)
5. ...

::: {.notes}
All important, if you have none of these I'd suggest this as a start
:::

# In code

## "Good code documents itself"

::: {style="font-size: 80%"}
```c
/// @brief Compute and return the root mean square (rms) error between
///        an expected value and the actual value.
/// @param n         Length of the vectors
/// @param actual    Computed values to compare
/// @param expected  Values expected from the computation
/// @return          Root mean square error
double rms_error(int n, int *actual, int *expected)
{
    double error = 0; // Running total of errors
    // Add contributions from each index to error
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        int error_i = actual[i] - expected[i];
        error += error_i * error_i / n;
    }
    return sqrt(error);
}
```
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a common and imo often misunderstood phrase.

Good code has comments where they are useful and has sensible naming to reduce the need for this where possible.
:::

## Comments

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=1}
::: {style="font-size: 80%"}
```{.c code-line-numbers="9-15"}
/// @brief Compute and return the root mean square (rms) error between
///        an expected value and the actual value.
/// @param n         Length of the vectors
/// @param actual    Computed values to compare
/// @param expected  Values expected from the computation
/// @return          Root mean square error
double rms_error(int n, int *actual, int *expected)
{
    double error = 0; // Running total of errors
    // Add contributions from each index to error
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        int error_i = actual[i] - expected[i];
        error += error_i * error_i / n;
    }
    return sqrt(error);
}
```
:::
Comments should enhance understanding - explain blocks,
describe confusing lines, justify workarounds, etc.
:::
::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
![Avoid redundant comments](https://i.redd.it/iuy9fxt300811.png)
:::
:::

## Docstrings

::: {style="font-size: 80%"}
```{.c code-line-numbers="1-6"}
/// @brief Compute and return the root mean square (rms) error between
///        an expected value and the actual value.
/// @param n         Length of the vectors
/// @param actual    Computed values to compare
/// @param expected  Values expected from the computation
/// @return          Root mean square error
double rms_error(int n, int *actual, int *expected)
{
    double error = 0; // Running total of errors
    // Add contributions from each index to error
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        int error_i = actual[i] - expected[i];
        error += error_i * error_i / n;
    }
    return sqrt(error);
}
```
:::
Docstrings give a structured way to define your interfaces
(and can be used to auto generate full reference docs!)

## VSCode extension
![Editor extensions can automate and use this kind of docs](./images/vscode-c-ext.png)

::: {.r-stack}
Time for a live demo :pray:
:::

## VSCode extension

Hopefully I remembered to show:

- Auto generate docstring boilerplate
- Show docs on hover
- Auto format (`alt + shift + f`)

## Git History

Always make useful commit messages

:heavy_check_mark: "Guard against negative length"

:x: "Bug"

<br>

- Short and informative
- Start with a verb (what does the commit do)
- Use the history (`git blame`, `git log`)

::: {.notes}
Hard to get a full team on board with this

Usually aim for a middle ground
:::

# Out of code

## Tooling

### [Doxygen](https://www.doxygen.nl/index.html)

- Generate reference documentation from docstrings!!
- [LAPACK docs](https://www.netlib.org/lapack/explore-html/) and [LAPACK docs source](https://github.com/Reference-LAPACK/lapack/blob/master/DOCS)

::: {.fragment}
### [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/)

- .rst or .md files to html
- [Python docs](https://docs.python.org/3/) and [Linux docs](https://docs.kernel.org/) (source code in link on each page)
:::

::: {.fragment}
### [MkDocs](https://www.mkdocs.org/), [Zeniscal](https://zensical.org/), ...
:::

::: {.notes}
Live demo!

- doxygen -g
- edit file: GENERATE_LATEX, PROJECT_NAME
- doxygen
- error message for undocumented function
:::

## Types of developer docs

- Overview/README
- Reference
- Install/Set up instructions
- Contributor guidelines
- Style guide
- Github/gitlab pull request checklists
- Git history (issues, pull requests, and commits)
- ...

## Types of user docs

- Overview/README
- Reference
- Install/Set up instructions
- Examples
- Tutorials
- Discussion boards
- ...

# Deploying

## [ReadTheDocs](https://about.readthedocs.com/)

- Automatically build and deploy sphinx docs
- Branches, releases, pull requests
- [ReadTheDocs' ReadTheDocs page](https://docs.readthedocs.com/platform/stable/tutorial/index.html)

## Github.io

- Host any HTML content
- Deploy with github actions
- [CCP-TEPP website](https://github.com/CCP-TEPP/ccp-tepp.github.io)

## CI/CD (in docs)

- Keep **published** docs up to date
- Check for build issues
- Highlight missing docs
- [Demo docs](https://github.com/AndrewLister-STFC/CCP-TEPP-Docathon-Presentation)

# Take aways

- Use Diátaxis to find gaps in docs
- Write reference docs with the code and use Doxygen, Sphinx, etc.
- Automate deployment of the docs
